essays.title: FILM APPRECIATION: JAYA JAYA JAYA JAYA HEY
essays.author: Dhanya KV | Research Scholar | Sri GVG Visalakshi College for Women

sections.content: Darshana Rajendran and Basil Joseph play the main parts in Vipin Das' 2022 Malayalam-language satirical drama Jaya Jaya Jaya Jaya Hey. The film's daring portrayal of domestic abuse cloaked in satire and black humour made it one of the most talked-about Malayalam films of the year. The film makes an impactful feminist statement about resistance, conditioning, and patriarchy through an apparently straightforward middle-class family story.

sections.heading: Overview of the Plot
sections.content: The protagonist of the tale is Jaya, a typical young woman brought up in a traditional home. She learns to adapt, make concessions, and put family honour ahead of her own goals from an early age. She is eventually married off to Rajesh, a small-scale businessman, after her education and aspirations are consistently neglected. Over time, layers of emotional and physical abuse are exposed in what starts off as a normal arranged marriage.

sections.heading: Social Commentary and Themes
sections.content: Fundamentally, middle-class Indian society's accepted patriarchy is criticized in Jaya Jaya Jaya Jaya Hey. The movie emphasizes how gender inequality starts in the home. Jaya’s father subtly discriminates against his daughter by prioritizing his son’s education while assigning Jaya household duties.

sections.heading: Characterization
sections.content: Jaya is portrayed by Darshana Rajendran in a complex and captivating way. She skilfully conveys her character's naivete, dread, annoyance, and ultimate resolve. Basil Joseph gives one of the most nuanced performances of his career as Rajesh.

sections.heading: Direction and Style of Narration
sections.content: Director Vipin Das balances comedy and seriousness effectively. The film’s fight scenes appear raw and authentic rather than glorified action sequences.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.content: Jaya Jaya Jaya Jaya Hey is a daring social critique of empowerment and patriarchy. Through powerful performances and sharp writing, the film challenges the normalization of domestic abuse and highlights female agency.

essays.title: HE DIED THE DAY HE FELT LOVED
essays.author: Allwin Rex K | St. Joseph’s College

sections.content: He was a man the world forgot. He was not evil and not cruel—only lonely. His heart was dry like an old leaf, ready to break with one small touch.
He walked through the town every day like a shadow. No one greeted him. No one cared if he was there. His silence became part of his life.
But one day something gentle entered his life. A lady approached him with kindness and tied a beautiful neck patch around his neck.
That small act of love awakened emotions he had never felt before. For the first time in his life, he felt warmth and belonging.
The next morning he did not wake up. He died peacefully with the gift in his hands and a small smile on his face.
Sometimes one small act of kindness is enough to give a lonely soul peace.

essays.title: A SPECIES SEARCHING FOR COMFORT
essays.author: Jaya Shri M | MA English Literature | St. Joseph’s College

sections.content: Nature provides the blueprint, humans reinterpret the design, and technology produces the replica. The evolution from wildness to civilisation to machinery reflects the journey of a species trying to balance beauty with survival.
Humans admire nature but cannot survive its intensity. Forests are admired for beauty but feared for wilderness. Rivers are pure but destructive. Animals are majestic yet violent.
To make life comfortable, humans reshaped nature: gardens instead of forests, homes instead of caves, taps instead of rivers, and zoos instead of wild animals.
Humans also copied nature. Drones were inspired by birds, vehicles replaced animals, and AI mimics the human brain.
Over time, humans began replacing nature not only out of intelligence but also because they could not endure the unpleasant side of nature.
What humans fail to understand is simple: humans are nothing without nature, but nature is everything without humans.

essays.title: The Pressure to ‘Fit In’: A Social Problem No One Talks About
essays.author: Sruthi G | B.Com Honours

sections.content: In today's student culture, the need to fit in has changed from a simple social instinct to a complex psychological burden. This pressure rarely gets discussed, but it greatly impacts the behavior and identity of young people.
Belonging is a basic human need, but in many college environments it comes with unspoken expectations about how students should speak, dress, and behave.
Social media intensifies this pressure by encouraging students to create idealized versions of themselves to gain approval through likes, shares, and comments.
Academically, the pressure to conform can limit intellectual curiosity. Students may avoid sharing unconventional ideas due to fear of judgment.
Colleges should promote inclusive cultures that celebrate individuality rather than conformity. Helping students embrace their authentic selves is essential for healthier academic communities.
